description: Fix port resolution bugs in CLI config set and plugin-tools build that cause server reload notifications to go to the wrong port, plus a missing FSWatcher error handler that crashes watch mode.

userStories.title: Fix handleSetPort sending reload notification to wrong port
userStories.description: As a user changing the MCP server port, I want the reload notification sent to the currently running server so that the server is properly notified of the config change.
acceptanceCriteria: After `opentabs config set port <new>`, the POST /reload is sent to the OLD port (where the server is actually running), not the new port
The `resolvePort(options)` call uses the old port value captured before the config write, not a freshly-read value from the just-written config.json
When `newPort === oldPort` (port unchanged), no 'Restart the MCP server' message is printed and no unnecessary notification is sent
userStories.notes:
File: platform/cli/src/commands/config.ts, lines 475-487.

Bug 1 (HIGH): `handleSetPort` writes the new port to config.json (line 472), then calls `resolvePort(options)` (line 478) to determine where to send the notification. `resolvePort` falls through to `readConfigPort()` (in platform/cli/src/parse-port.ts), which reads config.json from disk. Since the config was JUST written with the new port, `readConfigPort()` returns the NEW port. The notification goes to the new port (where nothing is listening) instead of the old port (where the server runs).

Fix: Capture `oldPort` before the config write (already done on line 464) and use `oldPort` directly as the notification port when `newPort !== oldPort`, instead of calling `resolvePort(options)`. If the user passed `--port` explicitly, that should override (they know which port the server is on). So: `const runningPort = options.port ?? oldPort`.

Bug 2 (LOW): The else-branch (line 484-487) fires when `newPort === oldPort` (port unchanged). It prints 'Restart the MCP server for the port change to take effect' and sends a notification — but no change occurred. Fix: Remove the else-branch entirely, or only print a 'Port is already set to X' message without the restart prompt.

Also on line 616: `if (!value)` uses JavaScript falsiness which catches the string '0'. Change to `if (value === undefined || value === '')` so that `opentabs config set port 0` falls through to `handleSetPort` which gives the proper 'Invalid port: 0' validation error.

userStories.title: Fix plugin-tools notifyServer ignoring config.json port
userStories.description: As a plugin developer using a custom config-based port, I want `opentabs-plugin build` to send the reload notification to the correct port so that the running MCP server discovers my plugin changes.
acceptanceCriteria: The `notifyServer()` function in plugin-tools reads the port from config.json (via @opentabs-dev/shared's getConfigPath) as a fallback when OPENTABS_PORT is not set
Port resolution order matches the CLI: OPENTABS_PORT env var → config.json port field → DEFAULT_PORT
userStories.notes:
File: platform/plugin-tools/src/commands/build.ts, lines 257-259.

The `notifyServer()` function only reads `process.env['OPENTABS_PORT']` and falls back to `DEFAULT_PORT` (9515). It does NOT read ~/.opentabs/config.json. The CLI's `resolvePort()` checks OPENTABS_PORT, then config.json, then default. If a user set a custom port via `opentabs config set port 3000` without the env var, the build command's POST /reload goes to 9515.

Fix: Add config.json reading as a fallback between the env var check and the default. Use `readFileSync(getConfigPath(), 'utf-8')` wrapped in try-catch (config may not exist). Parse JSON, extract the `port` field, validate it's a valid integer in 1-65535 range. This mirrors what the CLI's `readConfigPort()` does in platform/cli/src/parse-port.ts.

Import `getConfigPath` from `@opentabs-dev/shared`. The shared package is already a dependency of plugin-tools.

Do NOT import from @opentabs-dev/cli — that would create a circular dependency. Implement the config reading inline.

userStories.title: Add FSWatcher error handler in plugin-tools watch mode
userStories.description: As a plugin developer using watch mode, I want the watcher to handle filesystem errors gracefully so that deleting the dist/ directory doesn't crash my process.
acceptanceCriteria: The FSWatcher returned by `watch()` in the watch mode section has an 'error' event handler attached
If the watched directory is deleted or becomes unavailable, the process prints an error message and exits cleanly instead of crashing with an unhandled error
